title: Transformations of Graphs
blurb: One parent graph, four moves — shift, stretch, reflect, combine — and suddenly every function family is a single shape you already know, relocated.
buildsOn: Graph features and function notation from Unit 1.

lessons.title: Vertical & Horizontal Shifts
lessons.concreteComparison: A shift is picking a graph up like a sticker and re-placing it — same shape, new spot. Adding outside the function slides the sticker up; adding INSIDE the parentheses slides it left, the direction that fools everyone.
lessons.objective: Graph f(x) + k and f(x − h) as translations of a parent graph, and read h and k from a formula — including the counterintuitive inside sign.
concept: **Outside changes act on outputs**: $y = f(x) + k$ adds $k$ to every height, sliding the whole graph UP by $k$ (down if negative). The shape is untouched — every point moves the same amount.

**Inside changes act on inputs**: $y = f(x - h)$ shifts the graph RIGHT by $h$. It feels backwards, but follow one point: $f(x-3)$ produces $f$'s old value at $x = 0$ only when you stand at $x = 3$ — everything happens 3 units later, i.e. to the right.

The two combine freely: $y = f(x - h) + k$ relocates the parent's key point (a parabola's vertex, an absolute value's corner) to $(h, k)$. Track ONE landmark point and the rest of the graph follows rigidly.
lessons.keyIdea: Outside the function moves up/down as written; inside the parentheses moves left/right OPPOSITE to the sign: f(x − h) + k puts the landmark at (h, k).

facts.title: Vertical (outside)
facts.explanation: Outputs change; positive k up, negative down — as written.

facts.title: Horizontal (inside)
facts.explanation: Inputs change; x − 3 means right 3, x + 3 means left 3 — opposite the sign.

facts.title: Landmark rule
facts.explanation: Move one key point; the shape follows rigidly.

workedExamples.statement: Describe how to obtain $g(x) = |x - 4| - 2$ from the parent $f(x) = |x|$, and locate the corner.
workedExamples.solution: Inside: $x - 4$ shifts RIGHT 4. Outside: $-2$ shifts DOWN 2. The parent's corner $(0,0)$ lands at $(4, -2)$. Spot-check: $g(4) = |0| - 2 = -2$, the lowest output — corner confirmed.

workedExamples.statement: The parabola $y = x^2$ is shifted so its vertex sits at $(-3, 5)$. Write the new equation and find its $y$-intercept.
workedExamples.solution: Vertex to $(h,k) = (-3, 5)$: $y = (x - (-3))^2 + 5 = (x+3)^2 + 5$. Note the inside sign flip: LEFT 3 is written $x + 3$. Intercept: $x = 0$ gives $9 + 5 = 14$.

commonMistakes.text: Reading $f(x + 5)$ as a shift RIGHT 5 because the sign is positive.
commonMistakes.correction: Inside shifts run opposite the sign: $x + 5 = x - (-5)$ means $h = -5$ — LEFT 5. Test one point: the parent's x = 0 behaviour now happens at x = −5.

commonMistakes.text: Shifting the vertex but redrawing the arms at a new steepness.
commonMistakes.correction: A pure shift is rigid — the shape must be IDENTICAL, point for point. If the arms look different, you changed a stretch, not a shift.

tryIt.statement: Describe $g(x) = \sqrt{x + 1} + 6$ as transformations of $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$, and give the new starting point of the graph.
tryIt.solution: Inside $x + 1$: LEFT 1. Outside $+6$: UP 6. The parent starts at $(0,0)$; the image starts at $(-1, 6)$. Check: $g(-1) = \sqrt{0} + 6 = 6$. ✓

tryIt.statement: Write the equation of $y = x^2$ shifted right 2 and down 9, then find its $x$-intercepts.

steps.title: Graphs as stickers
steps.body: Here is the secret that collapses precalculus: there are only a handful of PARENT shapes — $x^2$, $|x|$, $\sqrt{x}$, $\frac{1}{x}$, and friends — and every variation you will ever graph is one of them picked up and re-placed like a sticker. This lesson is the two sliding moves; the next adds stretching and flipping.

steps.title: Slide the vertex
steps.teach: Step $h$ and $k$ and watch $y = (x-h)^2 + k$: the vertex goes exactly to $(h, k)$ while the cup keeps its shape. Now watch the FORMULA as you step $h$ right: it writes $x - h$. Right 3 reads 'minus 3' — hold that thought.

steps.title: The backwards-looking sign
steps.body: Why does $f(x - 3)$ move RIGHT? Chase one point. The parent $f$ does something special at $x = 0$ (a corner, a vertex). $f(x - 3)$ feeds the machine $x - 3$, which equals 0 only when $x = 3$ — the special moment now happens 3 units LATER on the number line. Inside changes preprocess the input, so the graph reacts opposite to the written sign.

steps.title: Which way?
steps.prompt: The graph of $y = f(x + 5)$ is the graph of $f$ shifted…
steps.explanation: Inside the parentheses, opposite the sign: $x + 5$ hits the parent's $x = 0$ behaviour when $x = -5$ — five units LEFT.

steps.title: Same V, new corner
steps.teach: Sample points of $y = |x - 4| - 2$: the parent V-shape of $|x|$, corner moved from $(0,0)$ to $(4, -2)$, arms still climbing at slope $\pm 1$. A shift changes WHERE, never WHAT.

steps.title: Track one landmark
steps.body: $y = f(x - h) + k$ does both moves at once, and the recipe is: find the parent's LANDMARK (vertex of $x^2$, corner of $|x|$, endpoint of $\sqrt{x}$, center of $\frac{1}{x}$), send it to $(h, k)$, and let the rigid shape follow. One point carries the whole graph.

steps.title: Build the equation from the vertex
Read the landmark
steps.prompt: Where is the starting point (endpoint) of $y = \sqrt{x - 7} + 3$?
steps.explanation: Inside $x - 7$: right 7. Outside $+3$: up 3. The parent endpoint $(0,0)$ lands at $(7, 3)$.

steps.title: Test the landmark numerically
steps.body: Whenever you announce a shift, plug the claimed landmark's x-value into the formula. For $y = (x+3)^2 + 5$ claiming vertex $(-3, 5)$: $f(-3) = 0 + 5 = 5$ ✓, and any neighbour gives more. Ten seconds, and the inside-sign trap never catches you.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Outside (+k): vertical, as written. Inside (x − h): horizontal, opposite the sign.
f(x − h) + k sends the parent's landmark to (h, k); the shape follows rigidly.
Verify every shift by evaluating the formula at the claimed landmark.

lessons.title: Stretches & Reflections
lessons.concreteComparison: Multiplying a graph is working dough: y = 2f(x) rolls it taller, y = ½f(x) presses it flatter, and a minus sign flips it over like a pancake. The shape stretches — it no longer moves rigidly.
lessons.objective: Graph y = a·f(x) as a vertical stretch/compression, handle the reflections y = −f(x) and y = f(−x), and read |a| and sign separately.
concept: $y = a \cdot f(x)$ multiplies every OUTPUT by $a$: heights double under $a = 2$ (a vertical **stretch**), halve under $a = \frac{1}{2}$ (a **compression**). Points ON the $x$-axis have height 0 and never move — the graph pivots around its own roots.

A negative sign is a mirror. **Outside**, $y = -f(x)$ flips every height: reflection across the $x$-axis. **Inside**, $y = f(-x)$ swaps left and right: reflection across the $y$-axis — inside changes act on inputs, as always.
Read $a$ in two parts: the SIGN says flipped or not, the SIZE $|a|$ says stretched ($|a| > 1$) or squashed ($|a| < 1$). $y = -3x^2$ is 'flip, then triple every depth': a narrow, downward cup.
lessons.keyIdea: a·f(x) scales outputs (x-axis points pinned); the sign of a flips over the x-axis, f(−x) flips over the y-axis — sign and size are separate decisions.

facts.title: Two mirrors
facts.explanation: Outside minus flips heights; inside minus flips left–right.

facts.title: Sign and size
facts.explanation: Decide the flip and the stretch separately.

workedExamples.statement: The point $(2, 6)$ lies on $y = f(x)$. Find its image on $y = \tfrac{1}{2}f(x)$, on $y = -f(x)$, and on $y = f(-x)$.
workedExamples.solution: Outputs scale or flip; inputs flip only for the inside minus. $\frac{1}{2}f$: $(2, 3)$. $-f$: $(2, -6)$. $f(-x)$: the height 6 now happens at $x = -2$, giving $(-2, 6)$.

workedExamples.statement: Describe $g(x) = -2(x - 1)^2 + 8$ as transformations of $x^2$, and find its $x$-intercepts.
workedExamples.solution: Right 1, stretch $\times 2$, flip over the x-axis, up 8: a downward cup with vertex $(1, 8)$. Intercepts: $-2(x-1)^2 + 8 = 0 \Rightarrow (x-1)^2 = 4 \Rightarrow x = 3$ or $x = -1$.

commonMistakes.text: Sliding the whole graph when multiplying: moving the roots of y = 2f(x).
commonMistakes.correction: A stretch is not a shift — points at height 0 stay EXACTLY put, because 2 × 0 = 0. Heights scale; roots are pinned.

commonMistakes.text: Treating −f(x) and f(−x) as the same flip.
commonMistakes.correction: The minus OUTSIDE flips heights (x-axis mirror); INSIDE it flips inputs (y-axis mirror). Test on (2, 6): they give (2, −6) versus (−2, 6) — different points.

tryIt.statement: $(−4, 10)$ lies on $y = f(x)$. Find its image on $y = -3f(x)$ and on $y = f(-x) + 2$.
tryIt.solution: $-3f$: height $10 \to -30$, point $(-4, -30)$. $f(-x) + 2$: input flips to $4$, then height $10 + 2 = 12$: point $(4, 12)$.

tryIt.statement: Write the equation of $y = |x|$ flipped over the x-axis, stretched vertically by 4, and shifted up 12; find where it crosses the x-axis.

steps.title: Working the dough
steps.body: Shifts relocate a graph rigidly. Multiplication actually DEFORMS it: $y = a f(x)$ takes every height and scales it by $a$ — taller dough for $|a| > 1$, flatter for $|a| < 1$, and a full pancake-flip when $a$ is negative. One anchor never moves: any point already sitting ON the x-axis, because scaling 0 gives 0.

steps.title: Stretch and flip y = ax²
steps.teach: Step $a$ through $2, 1, \frac{1}{2}$, then push it NEGATIVE. Watch two separate things: the SIZE of $a$ narrows or widens the cup, and the SIGN flips it upside down. The vertex, sitting on the x-axis, never budges.

steps.title: Pinned points
steps.prompt: $f(3) = 0$. On the graph of $y = 5f(x)$, the point at $x = 3$ is…
steps.explanation: $5 \cdot f(3) = 5 \cdot 0 = 0$. Stretches scale heights, and a height of zero scales to zero — roots are pinned.

steps.title: Two different flips
steps.body: Both reflections come from a minus sign, in two different places. **Outside** — $y = -f(x)$ — negates outputs: the graph flips over the x-axis. **Inside** — $y = f(-x)$ — negates inputs: left and right trade places, a y-axis flip. Same rule as shifts: outside acts on heights, inside acts on the input axis.

steps.title: Flip √x over the y-axis
steps.teach: Reflect these points of $y = \sqrt{x}$ across the y-axis. The image is $y = \sqrt{-x}$ — the same climbing curve, now living entirely on the LEFT, where $-x \ge 0$. An inside minus rewrites which inputs are legal.

steps.title: Name the mirror
steps.prompt: $(5, -2)$ lies on $y = f(x)$. Which point lies on $y = f(-x)$?
steps.explanation: Inside minus flips the INPUT only: the output $-2$ now occurs at $x = -5$. (Flipping the sign of the output would be $-f$, the other mirror.)

steps.title: Sign, then size
steps.body: For any coefficient, make two separate calls. $y = -3x^2$: the sign says FLIPPED (opens down), the size says STRETCHED (three times as steep — narrow). $y = -\frac{1}{4}x^2$: flipped and SQUASHED — a wide, shallow, downward cup. Never let the minus distract you from the magnitude.

steps.title: Why 'wider' equals 'shorter'
steps.body: A vertical compression by ½ produces a graph that LOOKS wider — and for parabolas it literally is one: $\frac{1}{2}x^2 = (\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}})^2$, a horizontal stretch by $\sqrt{2}$. For power functions the two deformations coincide; for most other functions they genuinely differ.

steps.title: What sticks
points: a·f(x): heights × a; x-axis points are pinned.
−f(x) mirrors over the x-axis; f(−x) mirrors over the y-axis.
Read a as two decisions: sign = flip?, |a| = stretch or squash?

lessons.title: Combining Transformations
lessons.concreteComparison: Reading a(x − h)² + k is reading dressing instructions: stretch and flip are the shirt (put on first, closest to the body), shifts are the jacket (last, outermost). Get the order wrong and the outfit — the graph — comes out wrong.
lessons.objective: Graph functions of the form a·f(x − h) + k by applying transformations in the correct order, and write equations from described transformations or graphs.
concept: The full form $g(x) = a \cdot f(x - h) + k$ bundles every move so far. Apply in this order: **(1) horizontal shift** by $h$, **(2) stretch/flip** by $a$, **(3) vertical shift** by $k$. The order matters because the stretch multiplies heights BEFORE $k$ is added — $-2f(x) + 3$ is not the same as $-2(f(x) + 3)$.

The safest execution is **landmark arithmetic**: take the parent's key point $(x_0, y_0)$; its image is $(x_0 + h, \; a y_0 + k)$. A second point (one unit from the landmark) fixes the shape: on the parent, one step gives rise $1$; on the image, one step gives rise $a$.
Going backwards — equation from a graph — read the pieces in reverse: the landmark position gives $h$ and $k$; the rise from the landmark to a neighbouring point gives $a$ (including its sign).
lessons.keyIdea: In a·f(x − h) + k, transform inside-out: shift horizontally, then stretch/flip, then shift vertically; a landmark point maps to (x₀ + h, a·y₀ + k).

facts.title: Order of moves
facts.explanation: Stretch before vertical shift — a multiplies f's output, then k is added.

facts.title: One step from vertex
facts.explanation: Read a (and its sign) from the first step away from the landmark.

workedExamples.statement: Graph $g(x) = -2(x + 1)^2 + 8$ by tracking the vertex and one neighbouring point of $y = x^2$.
workedExamples.solution: Landmark: $(0,0) \mapsto (0 + (-1), -2 \cdot 0 + 8) = (-1, 8)$. Neighbour: parent step from vertex rises 1; image step rises $a = -2$, so $(-1 + 1, 8 - 2) = (0, 6)$ is on the graph — confirmed by $g(0) = -2(1) + 8 = 6$. Downward narrow cup, vertex $(-1, 8)$.

workedExamples.statement: A graph is the parent $|x|$ flipped over the x-axis, stretched by 3, and moved to corner $(2, 5)$. Write $g(x)$ and compute $g(4)$.
workedExamples.solution: $a = -3$ (flip + stretch), $(h, k) = (2, 5)$: $g(x) = -3|x - 2| + 5$. Then $g(4) = -3|2| + 5 = -1$. Sanity: two steps from the corner at slope $-3$ drops $6$ from height $5$. ✓

commonMistakes.text: Adding k before multiplying by a: turning −2f(x) + 3 into −2(f(x) + 3).
commonMistakes.correction: In $a f(x) + k$, the stretch binds to $f$ FIRST; $k$ is added to the already-scaled height. Landmark check: $y_0 = 1, a = -2, k = 3$ gives $-2(1)+3 = 1$, not $-2(1+3) = -8$.

commonMistakes.text: Reading a's sign from the vertex position ('vertex is high, so a is positive').
commonMistakes.correction: The vertex position is pure (h, k) — it says NOTHING about a. Read a from the step away from the vertex: image falls where the parent rises means a < 0.

tryIt.statement: For $g(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{x - 4} + 1$: name the moves in order, the image of the parent's endpoint $(0,0)$, and $g(8)$.
tryIt.solution: Right 4, compress heights $\times \frac{1}{2}$, up 1. Endpoint image: $(0 + 4, \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0 + 1) = (4, 1)$. $g(8) = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{4} + 1 = 2$.

tryIt.statement: A parabola has vertex $(3, -4)$ and passes through $(5, 4)$. Write it in the form $a(x-h)^2 + k$.
tryIt.solution: $y = a(x-3)^2 - 4$. Use the point: $4 = a(5-3)^2 - 4 \Rightarrow 4a = 8 \Rightarrow a = 2$. So $y = 2(x-3)^2 - 4$.

steps.title: All four moves, one formula
steps.body: Everything so far compresses into one master form: $g(x) = a \cdot f(x - h) + k$. Shift, stretch, flip, shift — but in a specific ORDER, like dressing: the stretch $a$ is the shirt (applied to $f$'s output first), the vertical shift $k$ is the jacket (added last). Swap them and you get a different graph.

steps.title: Shirt before jacket
steps.prompt: $f$'s parent height at some point is $1$. With $a = -2$, $k = 3$, the image height is:
steps.explanation: In $a f(x) + k$ the multiplication binds to $f$ first: scale the height to $-2$, THEN add 3. Adding first computes a different formula, $a(f + k)$.

steps.title: The full family a(x−h)² + k
steps.teach: This workbench runs the complete form. Step $h$ and $k$ to place the vertex — then notice the arms keep their steepness. The vertex answers to $(h, k)$ alone; the shape answers to $a$ alone. Two independent dials.

steps.title: Landmark arithmetic
steps.body: Skip the mental animation — compute. The parent's landmark $(x_0, y_0)$ lands at $(x_0 + h, \; a y_0 + k)$. Then fix the shape with ONE neighbour: a single step from the parent's landmark rises 1 (for $x^2$ and $|x|$), so the image's single step rises exactly $a$. Two points, and the whole graph is determined.

steps.title: g(x) = −2(x+1)² + 8, plotted
steps.teach: The worked example's graph as sample points: vertex $(-1, 8)$ from $(h, k)$, and each step away drops by $2, 6, 10, \ldots$ — the $\times(-2)$ signature. Compare the parent's rises of $1, 3, 5$: same pattern, scaled and flipped.

steps.title: Equation from a graph
steps.body: Reverse-reading uses the same two facts in the other direction. The landmark's POSITION hands you $h$ and $k$ directly. Then step one unit horizontally from the landmark and read the rise — that rise IS $a$, sign included. If one step drops 3, then $a = -3$; done.

steps.title: Read a from a step
steps.prompt: A transformed $|x|$ has corner $(1, 2)$; the graph passes through $(2, -1)$. What is $a$?
steps.explanation: One step right of the corner, the parent rises 1; this image FALLS 3 (from 2 to −1). So $a = -3$: $y = -3|x-1| + 2$.

steps.title: Two-point verification
steps.body: Every combined-transformation answer earns a two-point check: evaluate your formula at the landmark (must give $k$) and at one step away (must give $k + a$). Both pass and the equation is almost certainly right; either fails and you know exactly which dial — position or shape — is off.

steps.title: What sticks
points: a·f(x−h)+k: horizontal shift, then stretch/flip, then vertical shift.
Landmark arithmetic: (x₀, y₀) → (x₀ + h, a·y₀ + k); one neighbour step reads a.
Backwards: landmark gives (h, k); the first step's rise gives a with its sign.

lessons.title: Piecewise & Absolute Value Graphs
lessons.concreteComparison: A piecewise function is a playlist with rules: THIS song plays before 6 pm, THAT one after. Each rule is an ordinary function; the art is switching at the right moment and knowing which endpoint owns the boundary.
lessons.objective: Evaluate and graph piecewise-defined functions with correct open/closed endpoints, and rewrite |x| expressions as piecewise formulas.
concept: A **piecewise function** applies different formulas on different stretches of the domain: $f(x) = \begin{cases} x + 3 & x < 1 \\ x^2 & x \ge 1 \end{cases}$. To evaluate, first ask WHICH rule owns the input: $f(0) = 3$ (first rule), $f(1) = 1$ (second — the $\ge$ owns the boundary).

Graph each rule only over its own territory, and mark the boundary honestly: a **closed dot** where the inequality includes the endpoint ($\le, \ge$), an **open circle** where it doesn't ($<, >$). If the two pieces meet at the same height, the graph is continuous there; if not, it JUMPS.

Absolute value is secretly the simplest piecewise function: $|x| = \begin{cases} x & x \ge 0 \\ -x & x < 0 \end{cases}$. Rewriting $|x - a|$ this way — split at $x = a$ — is how calculus will handle every absolute value it meets.
lessons.keyIdea: Evaluate piecewise functions by finding which rule owns the input; graph each rule on its own territory with honest open/closed endpoints; |x| is the two-rule function x / −x split at 0.

facts.title: Which rule?
facts.latex: f(1) \text{ uses the piece whose condition holds at } 1
facts.explanation: Check conditions before computing; exactly one owns each input.

facts.title: Boundary dots
facts.explanation: Closed dot = endpoint included; open circle = approached, not owned.

facts.title: |x| unpacked
facts.explanation: Absolute value is a two-piece linear function split at 0.

workedExamples.statement: For $f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x + 5 & x < -1 \\ x^2 - 2 & -1 \le x \le 2 \\ 6 - x & x > 2 \end{cases}$, evaluate $f(-3)$, $f(-1)$, $f(2)$, and $f(4)$.
workedExamples.solution: Route each input first. $-3 < -1$: $f(-3) = -6 + 5 = -1$. $-1$ belongs to the middle ($\le$): $f(-1) = 1 - 2 = -1$. $2$ also middle: $f(2) = 4 - 2 = 2$. $4 > 2$: $f(4) = 2$.

workedExamples.statement: Rewrite $g(x) = |x - 3|$ as a piecewise formula and evaluate both pieces' expressions at their shared boundary.
workedExamples.solution: Split where the inside is zero, $x = 3$: $g(x) = \begin{cases} x - 3 & x \ge 3 \\ 3 - x & x < 3 \end{cases}$. At $x = 3$ both expressions give 0 — the pieces MEET, which is why $|x-3|$ has a corner there, not a jump.

commonMistakes.text: Evaluating f(1) with every rule and reporting several answers.
commonMistakes.correction: Exactly ONE condition holds for each input — route first, compute second. If two conditions could both claim an input, the function is badly defined; well-written pieces never overlap.

commonMistakes.text: Drawing both boundary dots closed at a jump.
commonMistakes.correction: At a boundary, at most one piece owns the endpoint (its dot is closed); the other approaches it with an open circle. Two closed dots at one x would flunk the vertical line test.

tryIt.statement: For $f(x) = \begin{cases} -x & x \le 0 \\ x + 1 & x > 0 \end{cases}$: evaluate $f(-2)$, $f(0)$, and $f(0.5)$, and state whether the graph jumps at $x = 0$.
tryIt.solution: $f(-2) = 2$; $f(0) = 0$ (the $\le$ piece owns 0); $f(0.5) = 1.5$. Near 0 the right piece approaches $0 + 1 = 1$ while the function value is 0: heights 0 vs 1 — the graph JUMPS at $x = 0$.

tryIt.statement: Rewrite $h(x) = |2x - 6|$ as a piecewise formula, splitting at the right point.
tryIt.solution: The inside is zero at $x = 3$. For $x \ge 3$: $h(x) = 2x - 6$. For $x < 3$: $h(x) = 6 - 2x$. Check: $h(5) = 4$ both ways ($|4| = 4$); $h(1) = |{-4}| = 4 = 6 - 2$. ✓

steps.title: Different rules, different territories
steps.body: Real systems switch formulas: a taxi charges one rate for the first km and another after; a tax bracket changes at a threshold. A **piecewise function** writes that honestly — several formulas, each owning a stretch of the domain. The whole skill is routing: for any input, find WHOSE territory it is, then use only that rule.

steps.title: Who owns the boundary?
steps.prompt: $f(x) = \begin{cases} x + 1 & x < 2 \\ 10 - x & x \ge 2 \end{cases}$. What is $f(2)$?
steps.explanation: The condition $x \ge 2$ includes 2, so the second rule owns it: $f(2) = 8$. The first rule's $x < 2$ stops just short — a function gives ONE output per input.

steps.title: Honest endpoints
steps.body: Graph each piece only over its own territory, then tell the truth at every boundary: a **closed dot** if that piece's inequality includes the endpoint, an **open circle** if it only approaches. When neighbouring pieces meet at the same height the dots merge and the graph flows through; when they disagree, the graph JUMPS — and the dots show exactly which height is real.

steps.title: A jump at x = 0
steps.teach: Sample points of $f(x) = -x$ for $x \le 0$ joined with $x + 1$ for $x > 0$. Walk left to right: descend to $(0, 0)$ — the value the $\le$ piece owns — then the graph restarts a full unit higher at height 1 and climbs. That gap is a jump; no pen can draw it without lifting.

steps.title: |x| is piecewise in disguise
steps.body: Absolute value is the most famous piecewise function of all: $|x| = x$ when $x \ge 0$, and $|x| = -x$ when $x < 0$. Both pieces are plain lines (slopes $+1$ and $-1$); they meet at $(0,0)$ at different angles — that's the corner. To unpack $|expr|$, always split where the INSIDE equals zero.

steps.title: Distance from zero
steps.teach: Slide $x$ across zero and watch $|x|$: right of zero it reports $x$ itself; left of zero it reports $-x$, the flipped sign. Two linear rules, switching at the split point — you are watching the piecewise definition run.

steps.title: Split point
steps.prompt: To write $|4x + 8|$ piecewise, you split the domain at:
steps.explanation: Split where the inside changes sign: $4x + 8 = 0 \Rightarrow x = -2$. Right of it the expression is its own value; left of it, the negation.

steps.title: Test each piece at the seam
steps.body: After writing any piecewise formula, evaluate EVERY adjacent pair of expressions at their shared boundary. Equal values: the graph flows through (a corner at worst). Different values: a jump — and only the piece with the inclusive inequality gets the closed dot. This one habit prevents nearly all piecewise graphing errors.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Route the input to its owning rule before computing anything.
Closed dot = inequality includes the endpoint; open circle = approaches only; unequal heights = jump.
|expr| splits into two linear rules where the inside equals zero.

practice.statement: Describe the transformations taking $y = x^2$ to $y = (x - 6)^2 + 2$, and give the vertex.
practice.solution: Right 6 (inside, opposite sign), up 2 (outside, as written). Vertex $(6, 2)$.

practice.statement: The point $(3, 8)$ lies on $y = f(x)$. Find its image on $y = f(x + 2) - 5$.
practice.solution: Inside $x + 2$: left 2, so $x = 3 - 2 = 1$. Outside $-5$: height $8 - 5 = 3$. Image: $(1, 3)$.

practice.statement: $(−2, 6)$ lies on $y = f(x)$. Find its image on $y = -\tfrac{1}{2}f(x)$ and on $y = f(-x)$.
Write the equation of $y = \sqrt{x}$ reflected over the x-axis and shifted up 4; find where it crosses the x-axis.
Graph plan for $g(x) = 3|x - 1| - 6$: give the corner, the slope of each arm, and the $x$-intercepts.
practice.solution: Corner $(1, -6)$; arms have slopes $\pm 3$. Intercepts: $3|x-1| = 6 \Rightarrow |x-1| = 2 \Rightarrow x = 3$ or $x = -1$.

practice.statement: A parabola has vertex $(-2, 3)$ and passes through $(0, -5)$. Write it as $a(x-h)^2 + k$.
For $f(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & x < 2 \\ 3x - 5 & x \ge 2 \end{cases}$, evaluate $f(-3)$, $f(2)$, and $f(5)$, and decide whether the graph jumps at $x = 2$.
practice.solution: $f(-3) = 9$; $f(2) = 1$ (second rule owns 2); $f(5) = 10$. Near 2 the left piece approaches $2^2 = 4$, the actual value is 1: JUMP at $x = 2$.

testYourself.statement: Name the transformations taking $y = |x|$ to $y = |x + 3| - 7$, and find the corner and $x$-intercepts.
testYourself.solution: Left 3, down 7; corner $(-3, -7)$. Intercepts: $|x + 3| = 7 \Rightarrow x = 4$ or $x = -10$.

testYourself.statement: $(4, -6)$ lies on $y = f(x)$. Find its image on $y = -2f(x - 1) + 3$.
Which single reflection takes $y = 2^x$ (imagine its climbing curve) onto $y = 2^{-x}$, and what does it do to the point $(3, 8)$?
testYourself.solution: An inside minus: reflection across the y-axis. $(3, 8)$ maps to $(-3, 8)$ — indeed $2^{-(-3)} = 2^3 = 8$.

testYourself.statement: Write the equation of the parabola with vertex $(1, 10)$ passing through $(3, 2)$, and find its $x$-intercepts (exact form).
testYourself.solution: $y = a(x-1)^2 + 10$; point: $2 = 4a + 10 \Rightarrow a = -2$. So $y = -2(x-1)^2 + 10$; intercepts: $(x-1)^2 = 5 \Rightarrow x = 1 \pm \sqrt{5}$.

testYourself.statement: For $f(x) = \begin{cases} 4 - x & x \le 1 \\ x^2 + 2 & x > 1 \end{cases}$: evaluate $f(1)$ and describe the boundary dots at $x = 1$.
testYourself.solution: $f(1) = 3$ (the $\le$ piece owns 1): closed dot at $(1, 3)$. The right piece approaches $1 + 2 = 3$ — open circle at the SAME point, so the dots merge: the graph actually connects at height 3.

testYourself.statement: Rewrite $g(x) = |5 - x|$ piecewise, and compute $g(9)$ with the correct piece.
testYourself.solution: Inside zero at $x = 5$. For $x \le 5$: $5 - x$; for $x > 5$: $x - 5$. $g(9)$ uses the second: $9 - 5 = 4$. Check: $|5 - 9| = 4$. ✓
